coredata.dc:title: Theoretical study on electronic polarizability and second hyperpolarizability of hexagonal graphene quantum dots: Effects of size, substituent, and frequency
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Hai-Peng
Bi, Ze-Tong
Xu, Run-Feng
Han, Kui
Li, Ming-Xue
Shen, Xiao-Peng
Wu, Yu-Xi
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with unique and tunable physical properties have been the focus of considerable attention because of their potential for electronic and photonic applications. In this work, the electronic polarizability and second hyperpolarizability of hexagonal GQDs were investigated using density functional theory. The effects of size, substituent, and incident frequency of hexagonal GQDs on their electronic polarizability and second hyperpolarizability were comprehensively examined. Increasing the size of hexagonal GQDs enhanced the electronic polarizability and static second hyperpolarizability. The (hyper)polarizability of hexagonal GQDs was remarkably tuned by introducing electron donor/acceptor groups and incident frequency. This study provides new possibilities for practical applications of graphene materials in the nonlinear optical field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Electronic hyperpolarizability
Structure–property relationship
Frequency dependence

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 11504418, 11447033) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (Grant No. 2015XKMS075). We are also grateful to the Advanced Analysis and Computation Center of CUMT for the award of CPU hours to accomplish this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Polarizability and second hyperpolarizability and (b) frontier molecular orbital energy gap ΔE HL of different-sized hexagonal GQDs compared with benzene. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Electronic polarizability and second hyperpolarizability of pure and substituted GQD 2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Energy density of state (DOS) and molecular orbitals (MOs) of (a) pure GQD 2, (b) NH2-substituted GQD 2, (c) NO2-substituted GQD 2, and (d) NH2- and NO2-disubstituted GQD 2. In the DOS maps, each discrete vertical blue line near the abscissa axis corresponds to a MO, and the vertical black dashed lines highlight the positions of the HOMO and LUMO. The arrow denotes the HOMO to LUMO transition with the energy gap labled. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Relationship between electronic polarizability and second hyperpolarizability with energy gap ΔE HL for substituted GQD 2. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Electronic absorption spectra of pure GQD 2 calculated using the TDDFT method at CAM-B3LYP/6–31 + G(d,p) level of theory. The curve of the spectrum was plotted using the Lorentzian bandshape with full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 4000 cm−1. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Dispersion effects of (a)
| < α
( ω ; ω )
> |
in 10−40 C2 m2 J−1, (b)
| < γ
( − ω ; ω , 0,0 )
> |
in 10−61 C4 m4 J−3, and (c)
| < γ
( − 2 ω ; ω , ω , 0 )
> |
in 10−61 C4 m4 J−3 for GQD 2. The curves in the figures are fitted using the B-sample function. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Crucial vertical excitation properties of pure and substituted GQD 2 calculated using the TDDFT method at CAMB3LYP/6–31 + G(d,p) level of theory. λ max and f are the maximum absorption wavelength and oscillation strength, respectively. H and L denote HOMO and LUMO, respectively.

head.ce:title: Theoretical study on electronic polarizability and second hyperpolarizability of hexagonal graphene quantum dots: Effects of size, substituent, and frequency

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, China University of Mining and Technology, Xuzhou, 221116, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China University of Mining and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with unique and tunable physical properties have been the focus of considerable attention because of their potential for electronic and photonic applications. In this work, the electronic polarizability and second hyperpolarizability of hexagonal GQDs were investigated using density functional theory. The effects of size, substituent, and incident frequency of hexagonal GQDs on their electronic polarizability and second hyperpolarizability were comprehensively examined. Increasing the size of hexagonal GQDs enhanced the electronic polarizability and static second hyperpolarizability. The (hyper)polarizability of hexagonal GQDs was remarkably tuned by introducing electron donor/acceptor groups and incident frequency. This study provides new possibilities for practical applications of graphene materials in the nonlinear optical field.

Electronic polarizability and second hyperpolarizability of different-sized hexagonal Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) compared with benzene.
Image

ce:section.ce:para: New materials that exhibit nonlinear optical (NLO) properties are attracting intense interest in the scientific community because of their various potential applications in photonics [1,2]. Particularly, the high third-order NLO response is indispensable for achieving all-optical switching, modulating, and computing devices with modern optical technology [3]. Many previous studies have shown that donor/acceptor substituent, conjugation length, and dimensionality of the π-electron system could enhance the NLO properties of organic materials [4–8]. Significant progress has been made in NLO material research, but NLO materials have not yet reached the required standard for practical applications. Therefore, finding ways to increase the third-order NLO response of materials is still necessary to meet the needs for applications in the fabrication of various photonic devices.

Decreasing the size of semiconductor materials to the nanoscale level drastically changes their physical properties, resulting in unique properties due to their large surface area or quantum size effect [9]. Recently, theoretical and experimental studies have shown that graphene-based nanomaterials have superior third-order NLO properties because of their π-conjugated network systems, which have abundant π-electrons [10–13]. Among various nanographenes, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted increasing research interest because of its nonzero bandgap induced by quantum confinement and edge effects [14,15]. For example, the bandgap of GQDs can be tuned by simply changing their size during synthesis [16–19]. The fluorescent properties of GQDs can also be tuned because the emitted color depends on the size of the dots [20]. Although many studies reported the quantum confinement effect on the linear optical properties of GQDs, the relationship between molecular structure and third-order NLO properties of GQDs is still unknown [21]. Theoretical calculation of molecular structure and third-order NLO properties is important to understand the structure–NLO property relationship of GQD-based materials.

In addition, the NLO properties of molecular materials are strongly dependent on the microstructures and measurement conditions [22–25]. However, limited information is known about the size, substituent, and incident frequency effects of GQD-based materials on their third-order NLO properties, which triggered the interest in the subject. This work presents a systematic theoretical study on the electronic polarizability and second hyperpolarizability of small hexagonal GQDs by combining the first-principles density functional theory (DFT). This study aims to determine the influence of the size, substituent group, and incident frequency of GQDs on their electronic second hyperpolarizability. Results may provide valuable guidance for the design and synthesis of GQD-based NLO materials.

The second hyperpolarizability is the third-order response of the dipole moment with respect to the external electric fields [26]:
(1)
μ i
(
ω σ
)
=
μ i 0
+
∑ j
α
i j
( −
ω σ
;
ω 1
)
F i
(
ω 1
)
+
1 2
K
( 2 )
∑
j k
β
i j
k
( −
ω σ
;
ω 1
,
ω 2
)
F j
(
ω 1
)
F k
(
ω 2
)
+
1 6
K
( 3 )
∑
j k l
γ
i j
k l
( −
ω σ
;
ω 1
,
ω 2
,
ω 3
)
F j
(
ω 1
)
F k
(
ω 2
)
F l
(
ω 3
)
+ …
,
where
ω σ
=
∑ i
ω i
and the summations run over the field indices j, k, and l, which are associated with the Cartesian coordinates;
μ i 0
is one element of the permanent dipole moment; F
i is the i component of
F ⇀
; and α
ij , β
ijk , and γ
ijkl are the linear polarizability, the first hyperpolarizability, and the second hyperpolarizability tensors, respectively.
K
( 2 )
and
K
( 3 )
correspond to β and γ of different NLO processes that converge toward the same static value. For the third-order NLO responses, the static, electro-optical Pockels effect (EOPE), and electric-field-induced second harmonic generation (EFSHG) responses are expressed as
γ
( 0 ; 0,0,0 )
,
γ
( − ω ; ω , 0,0 )
, and
γ
( − 2 ω ; ω , ω , 0 )
, respectively.

For the static polarizability and second hyperpolarizability, the quantities of interest are the mean values derived using the following expressions:
(2)
< α > =
1 3
∑ i
α
i i
(
i = x ,
y ,
and
z
)
,
(3)

1 5
∑
i , j
γ
i i j j
(
i , j = x ,
y ,
and
z
)
.

The frequency-dependent <α> is denoted as:
(4)
< α
( − ω ; ω )
> =
1 3
∑ i
α
i i
( − ω ; ω )
(
i = x ,
y ,
and
z
)
.

The frequency-dependent <γ> is denoted as:
(5)
< γ
( − 2 ω ; ω , ω , 0 )
> =
1 5
∑
i , j
γ
i i j j
( − 2 ω ; ω , ω , 0 )
for
EFSHG
,
(6)
< γ
( − ω ; ω , 0,0 )
> =
1 5
∑
i , j
γ
i i j j
( − ω ; ω , 0,0 )
for
EOPE
(
i , j = x ,
y ,
and
z
)
.

The ab initio calculation method is an effective tool used to predict the electronic properties of compounds. The ground-state geometrical structures of GQDs were fully optimized using B3LYP functional [27] with the 6–31G(d) basis set. All optimized geometries were examined through vibrational frequency analysis, and no imaginary (negative) frequencies were observed, indicating that the equilibrium geometries are the stable conformations that correspond to the energy minima. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) were also computed at the B3LYP/6–31G(d) theory level. Ab initio techniques coupled with the finite field (FF) approach [28] are broadly applied to investigate the NLO responses because they can be used with various electronic structure methods to compute the NLO coefficients, including electronic second hyperpolarizability. Recent studies have shown that, for the evaluation of the NLO properties of carbon-based and silicon-based nanostructures, a long-range corrected DFT method, for example, CAM-B3LYP, can provide more reasonable and accurate predictions [22,29,30]. In the present study, the tensor components of electronic γ were calculated using the FF approach at the CAM-B3LYP/6–31 + G(d,p) level of theory. Selecting an appropriate differential step for the applied electric field is the prerequisite of using the FF method to investigate hyperpolarizability. The test study with a differential step of 0.0003 a.u. is suitable to attain reliable converged γ values for the investigated compounds. Moreover, the time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) procedure was employed to calculate the crucial electronic transitions of related systems, in which the time-dependent CAM-B3LYP/6–31 + G(d,p) method was adopted. These calculations were performed with the Gaussian 09 program packages [31].

Fig. 1 shows the static polarizability and second hyperpolarizability of benzene and different-sized GQDs, together with the HOMO–LUMO gap ΔE HL. With the increase in size of the GQDs, the energy gap ΔE HL is reduced, whereas the values of
< α >
and
< γ >
increase at different degrees; the extent at which
< γ >
increases is larger than that of
< α >
. Therefore, the energy gap in such clusters can be tailored by rational variation of cluster size. Similar results are also obtained in carbon clusters [16,18] and silicon clusters [22]. The present study indicated that the energy gap ΔE HL is strongly related to molecular NLO properties; an electronic system with large ΔE HL should be less NLO active than that with a small ΔE HL [32,33]. This result extends the understanding of the relationship between structures and third-order NLO responses of small GQDs.

GQDs possess good π-conjugation. When linked with electron donor/acceptor groups, the charge transfer is enhanced, leading to strong polarity [7]. This work calculated the electronic polarizability and second hyperpolarizability of electron donor/acceptor-substituted GQD 2, and the results are shown in Fig. 2 . The substituent group can enhance the (hyper)polarizability of GQDs because of the enhanced charge transfer induced by the substituent effect. For example, when moving from –NH2 substituent to –NO2 substituent and to –NH2 and –NO2 disubstituents, the value of
< γ >
increases by 119%–141% and to 177%, whereas the value of
< α >
increases by 105%–108% and to 114%, respectively, compared with the case of pure GQD 2.

Generally, the transition from HOMO to LUMO implies electron density transfer from the electron donor to the electron acceptor. Table 1 shows the crucial vertical excitation properties of pure and substituted GQD 2. The calculations show that electronic transition is not only dominated by HOMO to LUMO transition. Contributions from other transitions, such as HOMO to LUMO+1 transition (i.e., H→L+1) or HOMO−1 to LUMO transition (i.e., H−1→L), should also be considered. Fig. 3 shows the calculated energy density of state and molecular orbitals of pure and substituted GQD 2. It can be seen that the introduction of the electron donor/acceptor–substituted groups can reduce the energy gap ΔE HL. The GQDs exhibit intense low-energy absorption bands caused by intramolecular charge transfer excitation and thus contribute to large third-order NLO response [22,34]. Notably, a linear relationship exists between the calculated electronic (hyper)polarizability and ΔE HL (Fig. 4 ). The HOMO to LUMO transition shows the electron density transfer from the electron donor to the electron acceptor. The good linear fitting results indicate that the static electronic polarizability and the static electronic second hyperpolarizability increase with the decrease in the energy gap ΔE HL. This linear relationship exists not only in the present substituted GQD 2 but also in other series of π-conjugated compounds [7,35].

Next, the electronic absorption spectrum of GQD 2 was calculated using the time-dependent CAM-B3LYP/6–31 + G(d,p) method, and the absorption profile is shown in Fig. 5 . The maximum absorption wavelength λ max is approximately 271 nm for pure GQD 2, which is consistent with the theoretical value of approximately 269 nm [36] and the experimental value of approximately 261 nm [37]. Due to the absorption in the deep ultraviolet region, GQDs show highly promising potential for NLO frequency doubling applications. Fig. 6 illustrates the frequency dependence of the absolute values of
< α >
and
< γ >
of GQD 2. The dependences of
< α
( ω ; ω )
>
,
< γ
( − ω ; ω , 0,0 )
>
, and
< γ
( − 2 ω ; ω , ω , 0 )
>
on the incident frequency (or incident wavelength λ) are quite weak under off-resonant condition (λ > 700 nm). However, at λ
max = 271 nm, the resonance is enhanced in
< α
( ω ; ω )
>
,
< γ
( − ω ; ω , 0,0 )
>
, and
< γ
( − 2 ω ; ω , ω , 0 )
>
, which corresponds to the one-photon resonance effect. Moreover, the two-photon resonance enhancement at λ = 540 nm (≈2λ max) is clearly observed for
< γ
( − 2 ω ; ω , ω , 0 )
>
. According to the sum-over-states expression for the second hyperpolarizability [33],
< γ
( − ω ; ω , 0,0 )
>
(
< γ
( − 2 ω ; ω , ω , 0 )
>
) begins to disperse and exhibits a large value because of the one-photon (two-photon) resonance when
ℏ ω
(
2 ℏ ω
) is near the strong allowed transition energy. Similar conclusions were also reported [3,38]. Therefore, the third-order NLO properties of GQDs are strongly affected by the frequency of incident light and thus can be tuned using incident frequency for applications.

This study investigated the static and dynamic electronic polarizability and second hyperpolarizability of hexagonal GQDs using the DFT method. The effects of the size, substituents, and incident frequency of GQDs on their electronic polarizability and second hyperpolarizability were discussed. With the increase in the size of GQDs, the frontier molecular orbital energy gap decreases because of the enhancement of the electronic polarizability and static second hyperpolarizability. Furthermore, the introduction of the electron donor/acceptor can remarkably increase the electronic (hyper)polarizability of GQDs. Moreover, the frequency dispersion effect of electronic polarizability and second hyperpolarizability of GQDs were also determined. The third-order NLO properties of GQDs were strongly affected by the frequency of incident light and thus can be tuned by modifying the frequency. This study provides a practical guide for researchers to optimize the NLO properties of graphene-based nanomaterials for photonic applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 11504418, 11447033) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (Grant No. 2015XKMS075). We are also grateful to the Advanced Analysis and Computation Center of CUMT for the award of CPU hours to accomplish this work.
